sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 120, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE only changed the step counter text, no gameplay effect. Blue bar still at rows 12-15. Need to test if blue bar can move horizontally, or if MOUSE is needed.
- Plan: Move UP twice more to bring blue bar to rows 4-7, overlapping target rows 1-7.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 119 actions; a strong score needs about 39 or fewer. You are 3.1x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 62 actions returned the board to a state already seen 62 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 62 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The blue bar has been at many different positions throughout the game. Currently it's at rows 12-15, cols 28-47. The target is at rows 1-7, cols 36-39.
Looking at the history, the blue bar has been at rows 12-15 before, and also at rows 16-19, 20-23, 24-27, 28-31, 32-35, 36-39, 40-43, 44-47. So it can move both vertically and horizontally.
The key question is: what's the goal? The target is the charcoal+magenta object at the top. The blue bar needs to interact with it somehow.
Looking at the positions, the blue bar has been at rows 12-15, cols 36-55 before. That's close to the target's column range (36-39). Maybe I need to get the blue bar to overlap with the target.
Currently the blue bar is at rows 12-15, cols 28-47. The target is at rows 1-7, cols 36-39. If I move UP, the blue bar should go to rows 8-11. If I move UP again, it should go to rows 4-7, which overlaps with the target's rows.
But wait - I've been pressing UP and the blue bar hasn't moved from rows 12-15. Let me check if there's something blocking it. Actually, looking at the earlier history, the blue bar WAS at rows 12-15 before, and I pressed UP multiple times. Maybe it's stuck because of something.
Let me try LEFT and RIGHT to see if horizontal movement works. If the blue bar can move horizontally but not vertically, maybe there's a vertical obstacle.
Actually, looking more carefully at the positions, the blue bar seems to move in steps of 4 rows. From rows 12-15, moving UP would put it at rows 8-11, then 4-7. The target is at rows 1-7. So I need to get to rows 4-7 or possibly rows 1-4 (if that's possible).
But I've tried UP many times without success. Let me try a different approach - maybe I need to use MOUSE to click on the target or on the blue bar.
Actually, let me first try LEFT or RIGHT to confirm horizontal movement still works. If it does, then the issue might be that there's something blocking upward movement.
Wait - looking at the current position: rows 12-15, cols 28-47. The target is at cols 36-39. The blue bar already spans cols 28-47 which includes 36-39. So horizontally, the blue bar already overlaps with the target. The issue is just vertical alignment.
Let me try pressing UP again to see if it moves now. Maybe there was a temporary block that's been cleared.
Actually, I've been wasting too many actions. Let me think about this differently. The game seems to be about moving the blue bar to touch/overlap the target. The blue bar is currently at rows 12-15, cols 28-47. The target is at rows 1-7, cols 36-39.
Since the blue bar already spans the target's columns, I just need to move it up. But UP hasn't been working. Let me try MOUSE to click on the target directly, or maybe click on the blue bar to make it jump.
Let me try clicking on the target with MOUSE.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Blue bar has visited many positions (rows 12-47, cols 12-63). Currently at rows 12-15, cols 28-47. Target (charcoal+magenta) at rows 1-7, cols 36-39. Blue bar already overlaps target horizontally but is 5 rows below. UP hasn't been moving it. Need to try MOUSE or a different approach.
Plan: Try MOUSE to click on the target area to see if that triggers something.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Find target position and try clicking it
seg = current_frame.segmentation
target = None
blue = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c':  # charcoal - part of target
        target = n
    if n['color'] == 'b':  # blue bar
        blue = n

if target:
    # Get center of target
    rows = [p[0] for p in target['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in target['boundary']]
    center_row = sum(rows) // len(rows)
    center_col = sum(cols) // len(cols)
    print(f"Target center: row {center_row}, col {center_col}")
    print(f"Target boundary: {target['boundary']}")
    
# Try clicking the target center
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': center_row, 'col': center_col}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Target center: row 2, col 37
Target boundary: [[1, 36], [1, 39], [3, 39], [3, 36]]
